name: Engineering Fundamentals
description: The art and science of designing systems that work. From feedback loops to signal processing, engineering thinking helps you model, optimize, and debug complex systems — including your trading strategies.

nodes.name: Introduction to Engineering
nodes.description: What engineers actually do: breaking problems into pieces, iterating under constraints, and testing prototypes. Overview of major engineering disciplines and how they overlap with trading, programming, and quantitative finance.
keyConcepts: Engineering design process (define → ideate → prototype → test)
Systems thinking — seeing the whole, not just parts
nodes.whyItMatters: Engineering is applied problem-solving at scale. The same mindset — decompose, model, prototype, test — applies to building trading strategies, debugging code, and designing data pipelines. Engineering literacy turns you from a consumer of systems into a creator.
practiceProblems: Pick a household device. List 3 trade-offs in its design (cost vs quality, simplicity vs features, etc.)
Sketch a flow diagram for making a pour-over coffee. Identify inputs, outputs, and one failure point.
You're designing a bike helmet. List 4 constraints you must optimize within (weight, cost, safety, aerodynamics).
A swing bridge needs to open for ships but stay closed for cars. Sketch a simple mechanism and identify 3 potential failure modes.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Intro to Engineering
resources.whyHelpful: Free, interactive course covering engineering basics. Good starting point before diving into specialized fields.

resources.title: MIT: Engineering Systems Division
resources.whyHelpful: MIT's approach to engineering as a systems discipline. Advanced reading for understanding how complex engineered systems work.

resources.title: YouTube: What is Engineering?
resources.whyHelpful: 10-minute visual overview of what engineers do. Great for building intuition before textbooks.

resources.title: OpenStax: Engineering Mechanics
resources.whyHelpful: Free textbook covering statics, dynamics, and mechanics of materials. Used in university engineering programs. Deep but free.

quiz.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical engineering constraint?
quiz.explanation: Engineering always balances cost, time, safety, and performance. Aesthetics matters but never at the expense of these core constraints.

quiz.question: What does 'iterate faster' mean in engineering?
options: Build quick prototypes, learn from failures, and improve in cycles
Always use the most expensive materials
quiz.explanation: Iteration = build → test → learn → rebuild. Each cycle reduces uncertainty and improves the design. Skipping the test phase leads to catastrophic failures.

quiz.question: In a bridge design, you must minimize weight (materials cost) while maximizing load capacity. This is an example of:
options: A solved problem with one correct answer
A trade-off — improving one metric hurts another
quiz.explanation: Engineering is full of trade-offs. Lightweight materials cost more; strong materials are heavier. Good engineering finds the optimal balance given all constraints.

workedExamples.problem: Design a paper airplane that flies 20 feet. List 3 constraints and 3 design variables. Sketch a prototype and predict what will happen on the first throw.
workedExamples.solution: Constraints: must fit through a 3ft doorway, use only 1 sheet of 8.5x11 paper, no additional materials. Variables: wing span, weight distribution, center of gravity, wing curvature. Prototype prediction: narrow wings will dive; large wings will glide but be slow. First throw likely tail-heavy → stall. Fix: add a paperclip to nose for weight balance.
workedExamples.explanation: Every engineering project starts by defining constraints clearly. Paper airplane design teaches the same principles as bridge or rocket design, just faster iteration cycles.

workedExamples.problem: Your coffee maker breaks. You need hot coffee in 3 minutes. List the 5-step engineering process you would follow to solve this problem on a budget.
workedExamples.solution: 1. Define: hot coffee, ≤3 min, under $20 solution. 2. Ideate: run hot water over grounds in a sieve; use microwave heating; use hot tap water; build a rudimentary drip with a mug and filter. 3. Choose: hot tap water + pour-over is cheapest and reliable. 4. Prototype: buy a $4 pour-over dripper and $8 filters from grocery store. 5. Test: measure temp with kitchen thermometer, taste-test for extraction quality. Result: 90°C water, 2 min steep → excellent coffee. Works.
workedExamples.explanation: Engineering problems are rarely about having the right tools — they're about creatively applying constraints to find a workable solution. The pour-over hack uses physics (gravity, thermal mass) without any electronics.

nodes.name: Systems Thinking & Feedback Loops
nodes.description: Complex systems emerge from simple feedback rules. Learn to identify reinforcing loops (growth) and balancing loops (stability) — the same dynamics appear in ecosystems, economies, and market prices. Essential for trading system design.
keyConcepts: System archetypes: limits to growth, shifting the burden, tragedy of the commons
Delay effects — actions today have delayed consequences
Leverage points — where to intervene for maximum effect
nodes.whyItMatters: Trading strategies, portfolio drawdowns, and market crashes are all feedback systems. A stop-loss order that triggers selling during a crash can accelerate the decline (reinforcing loop). Recognizing these dynamics lets you design strategies that survive regime changes and avoid cascade failures.
practiceProblems: Draw a feedback loop diagram for a Ponzi scheme — what's the reinforcing loop that sustains it, and where does it eventually fail?
A rainforest temperature rises → less rain → more trees die → more CO2 in atmosphere → temperature rises further. What type of loop is this? What happens if the reinforcing loop breaks?
You add more traffic lanes to reduce congestion, but it attracts more drivers, eventually filling the lanes. What system archetype is this and what's a systemic solution?
Your trading account uses 10x leverage. A 10% loss becomes 100% drawdown. Is this a reinforcing loop? Design a balancing loop to prevent this.

resources.title: Thinking in Systems: A Primer (Meadows)
resources.whyHelpful: The definitive short intro to systems thinking by one of the best practitioners. Short, readable, life-changing for how you see the world.

resources.title: SD Reno: Systems Thinking Introduction
resources.whyHelpful: Practical intro to feedback loops, stocks/flows, and common system archetypes. Free and highly actionable.

resources.title: Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System
resources.whyHelpful: Meadows' most famous essay — identifies the most powerful (and least obvious) places to intervene in a system. Essential for trading system design.

resources.title: Iceberg Models — Root Cause Analysis
resources.whyHelpful: The Iceberg Model tool — events → patterns → structures → mental models — helps you get past surface symptoms to find root causes. Critical for trading drawdown analysis.

quiz.question: A positive feedback loop tends to:
options: Amplify change and create exponential growth or collapse
quiz.explanation: Positive feedback amplifies deviations. Population growth, bank runs, market bubbles, and viral spread are all positive feedback loops. They are inherently unstable and eventually hit a limit.

quiz.question: Which is an example of a balancing (negative) feedback loop?
options: A thermostat turning on heat when temperature drops below setpoint
quiz.explanation: Thermostats are classic negative feedback: they measure the gap between current and desired state, then act to reduce that gap. Trading strategies use similar thinking: if price deviates from moving average, mean reversion trades close the gap.

quiz.question: Adding lanes to a congested highway attracts more drivers, eventually filling the lanes again. This system trap is called:
quiz.explanation: Tragedy of the commons: shared resource (highway capacity) gets overused because each individual benefits from using it, while cost is distributed. Adding lanes temporarily relieves congestion, but induces demand until capacity is exhausted again.

workedExamples.problem: Draw a feedback loop diagram for a Ponzi scheme — what's the reinforcing loop that sustains it, and where does it eventually fail?
workedExamples.solution: Loops: R1: returns to early investors → recruiting → more investors → more returns. Balancing loop B: base of new investors shrinks → no new inflows → cash outflows exceed inflows → collapse. The scheme fails when the reinforcing loop runs out of new investor fuel. Total collapse is guaranteed by design — no productive output, only redistribution.
workedExamples.explanation: Ponzi schemes are reinforcing loops that require infinite growth. All reinforcing loops without a balancing counterpart eventually hit a wall — the question is when, not if.

workedExamples.problem: A rainforest model: temperature rises → less rain → trees die → more CO2 → temperature rises further. This is a dangerous reinforcing loop. Sketch a balancing loop that could protect the forest, and explain where the leverage point is.
workedExamples.solution: Balancing loop: temperature rise → international policy → emissions cut → CO2 reduces → temperature falls. Leverage point: the highest-impact place is the 'rules' paradigm — reframing the system's goals from 'economic growth at all costs' to 'regenerative limits'. Reducing CO2 directly is less effective than changing the decision-making structure. Cutting emissions helps but the systemic leverage is in shared mental models — which is why the challenge is political, not just scientific.
workedExamples.explanation: Climate messaging often targets symptoms (emissions reduction) not leverage points (goals and frameworks). High-leverage interventions are invisible and uncomfortable — that's why they're rarely chosen.

nodes.name: Signal Processing Fundamentals
nodes.description: Turn time-series data (prices, indicators, audio) into frequency-domain insights via Fourier transforms. Learn filtering, convolution, and how signal processing powers trading indicators, audio analysis, and image compression.
nodes.whyItMatters: Trading signals are just filters on price series. Moving averages = low-pass. RSI = oscillator (band-pass). Fourier analysis lets you see seasonality in prices before it hits the time-domain chart. A trader who reads spectral content can spot regime changes earlier.
practiceProblems: Compute the DFT of [1,0,0,0] — what does its spectrum look like.
A 5-day moving average is a low-pass filter. What cutoff frequency does it correspond to with 252 trading days/year?
A stock shows a 60-day cycle. Detect it with periodogram. Which window function prevents spectral leakage?
1-minute BTC data: sample at 5 minutes. What's the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing?

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Fourier Series
resources.whyHelpful: Visual explanation of Fourier series as rotating circles. Intuition-first approach — the best starting point before math-heavy lectures.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Fourier Transform
resources.whyHelpful: The Fourier Transform visualized. Connects circles to continuous spectrum. Essential for any trader or engineer.

resources.title: MIT OCW: Signals and Systems
resources.whyHelpful: Rigorous MIT course covering the full DSP toolkit. Start with the first 3-4 video lectures; the math is worth it.

resources.title: Richard Lyons: Understanding Digital Signal Processing
resources.whyHelpful: Practical DSP textbook — less abstract than Oppenheim & Schafer. Directly connects Fourier, filters, and sampling to real applications.

quiz.question: If you apply a low-pass filter to a price series, what does it do?
options: Smooth out noise, showing the slower trend component
quiz.explanation: Low-pass filters remove high-frequency (short-term) noise, preserving the long-term trend. A 50-day SMA is a simple low-pass filter — it smoooths but lags.

quiz.question: Nyquist theorem: you can accurately reconstruct a signal sampled at rate F if the signal contains no frequency above:
quiz.explanation: Fmax = Fs/2 is the Nyquist frequency. Higher frequencies alias (fold back) into lower frequencies. For 1-minute price data, cycles shorter than 2 minutes disappear from the record — you can't recover them.

quiz.question: In Fourier analysis, a sharp spike in the frequency domain represents:
options: A pure sine wave at that frequency
quiz.explanation: A spike in frequency domain = a pure sinusoidal component in time domain. If you see a spike at 60 days in BTC price spectrum, there's a genuinely 60-day cycle — not coincidence.

workedExamples.problem: Daily BTC prices for 3 years. Periodogram shows a strong peak at frequency 0.0036 (1/277). What does this mean?
workedExamples.solution: Period = 277 days ≈ 9-month cycle. Likely corresponds to Bitcoin halving events (every ~4 years). The peak is real but not directly tradeable on daily timeframe — it needs multi-year data to estimate and may shift. Use it as a regime-change detector, not a trade entry trigger.
workedExamples.explanation: Spectral peaks reveal hidden cycles. Even if you can't trade the long cycle directly, knowing dominant spectral components helps you distinguish signal from noise in your indicators.

workedExamples.problem: Apply a 10-day SMA to daily ETH/USD. Find -3dB cutoff freq. Draw impulse response.
workedExamples.solution: SMA(10) impulse: h[k] = 1/10 for k=0..9, 0 otherwise. Frequency response: rectangular window → sinc function. Main lobe first zero at f=0.1 cycles/day (10-day period). -3dB point ≈ 0.044 cycles/day (22-day period). Frequencies below 22-day are passed (barely); those above 10-day are attenuated -3dB; past main lobe zero they oscillate due to sidelobes. Analysts using SMA(50) effectively render a low-pass filter at 1-year cycle freq.
workedExamples.explanation: Filter design is the math behind moving averages. Understanding frequency response tells you exactly what time horizon your indicator responds to — and what information it throws away.

nodes.name: Control Systems: Stability & Response
nodes.description: Control theory: how to design systems that reach a target and stay there. Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controllers, stability margins, why over-aggressive gain causes oscillation. Applies to algo tuning and portfolio rebalancing.
keyConcepts: System stability: Bode plots, phase margin, gain margin
Overshoot, rise time, and steady-state error
nodes.whyItMatters: A trading bot that reacts too aggressively overtrades and blows up; too slowly misses opportunities. Control theory tells you how to tune gains to get desired response: overshoot <5%, settling time <1hr. The same math that lands rockets keeps your bot profitable.
practiceProblems: You have a P-only controller with proportional gain. Its output oscillates at ±10% around setpoint. What do you increase to reduce oscillations? What's the risk of simply increasing P gain?
A heating system has gain 2.0 and is unstable. If you add integral action, what happens? When does integral windup occur and how do you prevent it?
Your trading algo has 1-second response time. You want it to settle within 2 seconds when price moves, with <2% overshoot. What type of controller should you use and why?
Draw a Bode plot for a first-order system with time constant τ=5 seconds. Estimate the phase margin and predict if it will oscillate.

resources.title: Brian Douglas: Control System Lectures
resources.whyHelpful: Best free video series on control theory. Visual intuition. Each video is 5-15 minutes. Cover all basics before any textbook.

resources.title: MIT 6.003: Feedback Systems
resources.whyHelpful: MIT's systems and control course. More math-heavy than Douglas videos but much deeper.

resources.title: PID Control Fundamentals
resources.whyHelpful: Practical PID tuning guide. Directly applicable to tuning algorithmic trading parameters.

resources.title: Stability in Control Systems (MathWorks)
resources.whyHelpful: Industry-standard explanations of stability, Bode plots, and Nichols charts. Good reference for connecting math to real systems.

quiz.question: In a PID controller, what does the Derivative term respond to?
options: Rate of change of error (how fast you're moving toward target)
quiz.explanation: P = present error (where you are). I = past accumulated error (how long you've been off). D = predicted future error based on rate of change — if you're heading toward target too fast, D acts as a brake.

quiz.question: A P-only controller oscillates around the setpoint without ever settling. What's the likely fix?
options: Add Derivative or Integral action, or lower P gain
Increase the setpoint to reduce error
quiz.explanation: High P gain causes instability (oscillations). Derivative adds damping (slows approach); Integral removes steady-state error. Lowering P is the quickest fix before adding I or D.

quiz.question: What is 'integral windup' and how do you prevent it?
options: The integral term growing without bound when the actuator is saturated — prevented with anti-windup clamping
The derivative term getting too large in noisy conditions
A stability criterion requiring integral phase lag < 90
When the setpoint changes faster than the controller can respond
quiz.explanation: Integral windup: when output is saturated but error persists, the integral accumulates unbounded, causing massive overshoot when constraint releases. Fix: clamp the integral term while actuator is saturated.

workedExamples.problem: Tuning a portfolio rebalancing bot. P-only overshoots 15% and oscillates every 2 hours. Add D gain and predict overshoot change. Safe D gain range for 1-hour rebalance period?
workedExamples.solution: Problem: too much P gain, no damping. Add D = Kd * d(error)/dt. With Kd = 0.5*Kp on first hour: derivative reduces output as error shrinks, reducing overshoot by ~25% at cost of ~10% slower settling. Safe Kd: 4-8 hours derivative time for 1-hour rebalance (TD=Kd/Kp). Start with Kd=0.5; avoid Kd>0.8 or you'll exceed the stability margin.
workedExamples.explanation: Overshooting means paying slippage and fees. Control theory gives bounds. Many real bots use fixed rebalance schedule to avoid this; if continuous, control theory keeps you stable.

workedExamples.problem: SpaceX Falcon 9: P-only causes high-frequency oscillation. Adding D gives sluggishness. Need: rise time <2s, overshoot <2%, steady-state error <0.1°. For which controller? Bode tune?
workedExamples.solution: Use PID with appropriate gains. For rise time <2s set ω_c ≈ 3 rad/s. For overshoot <2% set phase margin φ_m ≈ 70° by adding ~40° with lead compensator. Gains: Kp=2.5, Ki≈2 sec^-1, Kd≈0.83 sec. Predicted specs: rise time ~1.8s, settling time ~4s, steady-state error <0.05°. Real SpaceX uses full nonlinear model-predictive control, but the core PID + filtering is the same.
workedExamples.explanation: Falcon 9 uses PID in the gimbal control loop for stabilization. Control theory is what makes reusable rockets possible. This is the same math for your trading bot.
